name: Power BI & Tableau
blurb: Data models, DAX filter context, and visuals that don’t lie.

lessons.title: From Raw Data to a Star: How Power BI Actually Thinks
lessons.concept:
Every Power BI report follows the same four-beat rhythm, and beginners who skip a beat suffer for weeks. First, Get data: you connect to a source (Excel, SQL, a CSV export from your ERP) and clean it in Power Query, the transform layer where you rename columns, fix data types, remove junk rows and split fields. Second, Model: you arrange your tables and draw relationships between them. Third, DAX: you write calculations. Fourth, Visualise: you drag fields onto the canvas. The mistake almost everyone makes is rushing to beat four while ignoring beat two. The model is the engine; the charts are just the dashboard needle.

The gold-standard model shape is the star schema. Picture a large retailer like DMart. At the centre sits a fact table, Sales, with one row per invoice line item, say 2 crore rows, holding keys and numbers (Qty, Amount). Around it sit dimension tables, the descriptive 'who/what/when/where': Dim_Product (one row per SKU), Dim_Store (one row per outlet), Dim_Customer, and Dim_Date. Drawn out, the fact is the hub and the dimensions are the points of a star, joined by one-to-many relationships, one product row relating to many sales rows.

Why a star and not one giant flat table? Because filters flow downhill, from the 'one' side (dimension) to the 'many' side (fact), in a single default direction. When a user clicks 'Maharashtra' on a Store slicer, that filter travels down the relationship and trims the Sales fact automatically. The star also compresses beautifully in Power BI's VertiPaq engine and keeps DAX simple. Microsoft explicitly recommends it, so make the star your default, not your afterthought.
lessons.example: A Pune-based grocery chain exports 2 crore sales rows. Instead of one 60-column spreadsheet, they build Fact_Sales (keys + Qty + Amount) linked to Dim_Product, Dim_Store, Dim_Customer and Dim_Date. Now a manager filters 'Category = Staples' and 'Month = July 2026' with two slicer clicks, and every visual updates because filters travel from the dimensions into the fact.
lessons.code:
Fact_Sales  (grain = 1 row per invoice line)
InvoiceNo | DateKey  | ProductKey | StoreKey | Qty | Amount
INV1001   | 20260718 | P-450      | S-12     |  3  | 1497
INV1001   | 20260718 | P-981      | S-12     |  1  |  240

Dim_Product (grain = 1 row per SKU)
ProductKey | Product         | Category | Brand
P-450      | Aashirvaad 5kg  | Staples  | ITC
P-981      | Colgate 200g    | Personal | Colgate

Relationship: Dim_Product[ProductKey] 1 --- * Fact_Sales[ProductKey]  (single direction)
lessons.tryit: In Power BI Desktop, load two CSVs, a Sales file (with ProductKey, Qty, Amount) and a Product file (ProductKey, Product, Category). Go to Model view, drag ProductKey from Product onto ProductKey in Sales to make a one-to-many relationship, then drop a Category slicer and a Sum of Amount card and confirm the card reacts to the slicer.

mistakes.m: Loading one wide 'flat' table with everything in it and calling it a model.
mistakes.fix: Split events from descriptions: a thin fact table of transactions plus separate dimension tables for Product, Store, Customer and Date.

mistakes.m: Cleaning data by typing over cells or adding helper columns in Excel before import.
mistakes.fix: Do all shaping in Power Query so the steps are repeatable on every refresh, not a one-time manual edit.

mistakes.m: Joining fact-to-fact or setting relationships to bi-directional 'to be safe'.
mistakes.fix: Keep dimensions on the 'one' side, the fact on the 'many' side, and leave the cross-filter direction as single unless you have a proven reason.

quiz.q: In a retailer's model, which table is the fact table?
opts: Dim_Product, one row per SKU
Dim_Date, one row per calendar day
Fact_Sales, one row per invoice line item
Dim_Store, one row per outlet
quiz.why: A fact table records events or transactions at a fine grain, one row per invoice line here, and holds the numeric measures (Qty, Amount) plus keys. The other three are dimensions: descriptive lookup tables with one row per entity.
quiz.approach: Ask 'is this an event I measure, or a thing I describe?' Events become fact tables; things become dimensions.

quiz.q: By default, in which direction do filters flow across a one-to-many relationship?
opts: From the many-side fact to the one-side dimension
From the one-side dimension to the many-side fact
In both directions equally
Filters never cross relationships
quiz.why: Power BI's default single cross-filter direction sends filters from the 'one' side (dimension) down to the 'many' side (fact). That is exactly why clicking a value on a dimension slicer trims the fact table and updates your visuals.
quiz.approach: Design so users slice on dimensions and let the filter cascade into the fact; avoid bi-directional filtering unless a specific need forces it.

quiz.q: Where should you rename columns, fix data types and remove junk rows?
opts: In DAX measures after loading
In Power Query, before loading to the model
Directly on the report canvas
In the source Excel file each time by hand
quiz.why: Power Query is the repeatable transform layer: its steps re-run automatically on every refresh, so your cleaning survives new data. DAX is for calculations on already-clean data, and manual Excel edits are lost on the next export.
quiz.approach: Shape and clean in Power Query first; treat DAX and visuals as steps that assume the data is already tidy.

lessons.title: Measure vs Calculated Column, and the Magic of SUMX
lessons.concept:
This is the single most misunderstood idea for new Power BI users, and getting it right instantly makes you look senior. A calculated column is computed row by row when the data refreshes, and the result is physically stored in the model, taking up memory and file size. It has row context, meaning it can see the other columns of its own row. A measure is different: it is computed on the fly at query time, in whatever filter context the cell it lands in provides, and it stores nothing. Click a slicer and every measure recalculates; a calculated column just sits there, frozen from the last refresh.

So which do you use? The rule of thumb: if the value will sit on an axis, a slicer, a legend or a filter, it must exist per row, so use a calculated column (for example a 'Price Band' bucket or a 'Metro / Non-metro' flag). If the value is an aggregation that must respond to the user's filters, Total Sales, Margin %, Average Order Value, use a measure. When in doubt, default to a measure. Beginners who make everything a calculated column end up with bloated, slow files.

Now the star of aggregation: SUMX. Plain SUM works on one existing column: SUM(Sales[Amount]). But suppose your fact has Qty and Unit Price separately and no Amount column. You cannot SUM a multiplication that does not exist as a stored column. SUMX is an iterator: SUMX(Sales, Sales[Qty] * Sales[Unit Price]) walks the table row by row, evaluates Qty times Price for each row, then adds up the results. You could instead create a calculated column and SUM it, but SUMX gives the same answer without storing an extra column, keeping the model smaller and faster.
lessons.example: A Bengaluru D2C brand sells on its own site. Their order-line table has Qty and Unit Price but no line total. Total Revenue = SUMX(Orders, Orders[Qty] * Orders[Unit Price]) multiplies each of, say, 5 lakh rows and sums them. Because it is a measure, the same formula returns July revenue when a July slicer is on, and Diwali-week revenue when that slicer is on, no rewrite needed.
lessons.code:
-- Calculated COLUMN: stored per row, uses row context
Line Total = Sales[Qty] * Sales[Unit Price]

-- A measure summing that stored column
Total Sales = SUM ( Sales[Line Total] )

-- Better: skip the stored column, iterate instead
Total Sales = SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Qty] * Sales[Unit Price] )

-- Margin %, DIVIDE guards against divide-by-zero
Margin % = DIVIDE ( [Total Profit], [Total Sales] )
lessons.tryit: In a Sales table that has Qty and Unit Price but no total, create a measure Total Revenue = SUMX(Sales, Sales[Qty] * Sales[Unit Price]). Put it in a card, add a Month slicer, and confirm the number changes as you pick different months, proof it is a measure responding to filter context.

mistakes.m: Building 'Total Sales' as a calculated column and wondering why it will not respond to slicers.
mistakes.fix: Aggregations that must react to user filters should be measures, which recompute in the current filter context on every click.

mistakes.m: Writing SUM(Sales[Qty] * Sales[Unit Price]) and getting an error.
mistakes.fix: SUM takes exactly one column, not an expression. Use the iterator SUMX(Sales, Sales[Qty] * Sales[Unit Price]) to multiply row by row then sum.

mistakes.m: Adding a calculated column for every little derived value 'because it is easier'.
mistakes.fix: Each stored column inflates the model and slows refresh; prefer measures and iterators, and reserve columns for values you must slice or group by.

quiz.q: You need a 'Total Sales' KPI card that updates whenever the user picks a Region on a slicer. What should it be?
opts: A calculated column, so it is stored and fast
A measure, so it recomputes in the current filter context
A Power Query custom column
A calculated table
quiz.why: Only measures recalculate at query time inside the filter context of the cell, so a slicer click re-evaluates them. A calculated column is frozen at the last refresh and cannot react to slicers.
quiz.approach: Aggregations that must respond to filters are always measures; default to measures unless you specifically need a per-row value to slice by.

quiz.q: Your fact has Qty and Unit Price but no line-amount column. Which correctly totals revenue?
opts: SUM(Sales[Qty] * Sales[Unit Price])
SUMX(Sales, Sales[Qty] * Sales[Unit Price])
SUM(Sales[Qty]) * SUM(Sales[Unit Price])
AVERAGE(Sales[Unit Price])
quiz.why: SUMX iterates row by row, computing Qty times Price for each line before summing, which is exactly what revenue needs. SUM cannot take an expression, and multiplying the two grand totals (option 3) mixes unrelated quantities and gives a wildly wrong number.
quiz.approach: When the calculation must happen at the row level before aggregating, reach for an X-iterator like SUMX rather than SUM.

quiz.q: Which statement about storage is correct?
opts: Measures are stored in the model; calculated columns are not
Calculated columns are stored at refresh; measures are computed at query time
Both are stored on disk identically
Neither consumes any memory
quiz.why: A calculated column is materialised row by row during refresh and physically stored, adding to file size. A measure stores no values and is evaluated only when a visual asks for it, in that visual's filter context.
quiz.approach: Favour measures to keep models lean; add calculated columns only when you genuinely need a stored, sliceable per-row value.

lessons.title: CALCULATE: The One Function That Bends Filters (plus Time Intelligence)
lessons.concept:
To go beyond basic totals you must understand filter context: the complete set of filters acting on a cell right now, coming from slicers, the row and column headers of a matrix, and other visuals. A measure like [Total Sales] simply reports the sum for whatever filter context surrounds it, one value in the 'Maharashtra, July' cell, a different value in the 'Karnataka, August' cell. CALCULATE is the one and only DAX function that can change that filter context. Its shape is CALCULATE(expression, filter1, filter2, ...): it evaluates the expression after adding or overriding the filters you list. So CALCULATE([Total Sales], Region[State] = "Maharashtra") returns Maharashtra sales even in a row that is not about Maharashtra.

To compute shares and ratios you often need to remove filters, to build a denominator. ALL and its clearer modern twin REMOVEFILTERS strip filters away. A percent-of-total is DIVIDE([Total Sales], CALCULATE([Total Sales], REMOVEFILTERS(Region))): the numerator respects the current region, the denominator ignores region and gives the all-regions total. Beginners misread ALL as 'delete the data', it does the opposite: it clears filters so more rows become visible, which is precisely what a grand-total denominator needs.

Time intelligence turns dates into growth stories, but it depends on a proper Date table: a dedicated dimension with one contiguous row per day, marked as a date table. Then YTD is TOTALYTD([Total Sales], 'Date'[Date]); month-to-date uses DATESMTD; and last year's figure is CALCULATE([Total Sales], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR('Date'[Date])). A neat India-specific touch: because our fiscal year runs April to March, TOTALYTD accepts a year-end-date argument so your YTD resets on 1 April, not 1 January.
lessons.example: A Flipkart-style marketplace tracks Big Billion Days growth. In a monthly matrix, [Total Sales] shows each month, [Sales Last Year] uses SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR to pull the same month a year earlier, and YoY % shows +28% for October. A '% of All Categories' measure with REMOVEFILTERS(Category) shows Electronics is 41% of the festival's revenue, all from the same base [Total Sales] measure.
lessons.code:
Total Sales = SUMX ( Sales, Sales[Qty] * Sales[Unit Price] )

-- Year-to-date on the Indian fiscal year (resets 1 April)
Sales YTD =
TOTALYTD ( [Total Sales], 'Date'[Date], "3/31" )   -- year-end 31 March; string format is locale-dependent

-- Same period last year
Sales LY =
CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR ( 'Date'[Date] ) )

YoY % = DIVIDE ( [Total Sales] - [Sales LY], [Sales LY] )

-- Share of grand total across regions
% of All Regions =
DIVIDE ( [Total Sales], CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], REMOVEFILTERS ( Region ) ) )
lessons.tryit: Create a Date dimension (or mark your date table as a Date Table), then build a matrix with Month on rows and add three measures: [Total Sales], [Sales LY] using SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR, and YoY %. Confirm each month lines up against the same month last year.

mistakes.m: Running time intelligence off the date column inside the fact table.
mistakes.fix: Time-intelligence functions need a dedicated Date dimension with contiguous daily rows, marked as a date table; connect it to the fact and use its date column.

mistakes.m: Believing ALL or REMOVEFILTERS deletes rows or empties the visual.
mistakes.fix: They remove filters, not data, widening the context so you get a stable denominator like the grand total for a percentage.

mistakes.m: Nesting several plain filters and expecting them to add up instead of override.
mistakes.fix: A CALCULATE filter on a column overrides the existing filter on that same column; use ALL/KEEPFILTERS deliberately when you want different behaviour.

quiz.q: Which DAX function can modify the filter context in which an expression is evaluated?
opts: SUMX
CALCULATE
RELATED
AVERAGE
quiz.why: CALCULATE is unique in DAX: it evaluates its expression after applying the filter arguments you give, adding or overriding the surrounding filter context. SUMX only iterates, RELATED just fetches a related value, and AVERAGE only aggregates.
quiz.approach: Whenever you need 'the same measure but under different filters', CALCULATE is the tool; learn it deeply before anything else in DAX.

quiz.q: To compute each region's share of the grand total, what belongs in the denominator?
opts: SUM of a calculated column of shares
CALCULATE([Total Sales], REMOVEFILTERS(Region))
[Total Sales] with no changes
SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR('Date'[Date])
quiz.why: The denominator must ignore the current region so it equals the all-regions total. REMOVEFILTERS(Region) (like ALL) clears the region filter, giving that stable grand total, while the numerator keeps the current region's sales.
quiz.approach: For any part-of-whole percentage, strip the filter on the dimension you are dividing by using ALL or REMOVEFILTERS in the denominator.

quiz.q: What does SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR require to work correctly?
opts: A measure written as a calculated column
A contiguous, marked Date table and a date column
Bi-directional relationships everywhere
The fact table sorted by amount
quiz.why: Time-intelligence functions shift a set of dates, so they need a proper Date dimension with an unbroken run of daily dates (ideally marked as a date table). Without it, the year-shift is unreliable or errors out.
quiz.approach: Build a dedicated Date table early; it is the foundation for every YTD, MTD and year-over-year comparison.

lessons.title: Tableau in a Day: Dimensions, Measures, LOD FIXED and Dashboards
lessons.concept:
Tableau splits every field into two roles the moment you connect data. Dimensions are qualitative fields that slice and categorise, Region, Product, Order Date, and Tableau shows them as blue, discrete pills by default. Measures are quantitative fields that get aggregated, Sales, Profit, Quantity, shown as green, continuous pills that default to SUM. You build a chart by dragging pills onto Rows and Columns, and Tableau automatically aggregates each measure at the 'level of detail' set by the dimensions in the view: put Region on Rows and Sales on Columns and you get sum of sales per region. Remember the two axes of jargon are separate: dimension-versus-measure is the data role, while discrete-blue-versus-continuous-green is how it is displayed, and you can convert between them.

Sometimes you need a number computed at a different granularity than the view shows, and that is what Level of Detail (LOD) expressions are for, FIXED being the workhorse. { FIXED [Customer ID] : SUM([Sales]) } computes total sales per customer regardless of what dimensions sit in the view. It is perfect for 'sales per customer' or cohort metrics that you then average. The key rule: FIXED ignores the view's dimensions (though it does respect context filters), whereas INCLUDE and EXCLUDE adjust granularity relative to the view. In Tableau's order of operations, context filters apply before FIXED, and ordinary dimension filters apply after it.

Finally, dashboards make it interactive. A dashboard stitches several sheets onto one canvas, and dashboard actions wire them together: a Filter action lets a click on a bar in one sheet filter the others, a Highlight action dims everything except the selection, and a URL action jumps to a web page. Pair this with sound visual choice, bars to compare categories, lines for trends over time, scatter for correlation, and avoid pie charts beyond two or three slices, and mind performance by keeping the number of sheets and marks per dashboard lean.
lessons.example: A Delhi cloud-kitchen brand analyses Swiggy and Zomato orders in Tableau. A FIXED LOD, { FIXED [Customer ID] : SUM([Sales]) }, gives lifetime spend per customer, which they average to find AOV per repeat customer is ₹512. On a dashboard, clicking a city bar (Filter action) instantly refreshes the dish-level chart and the trend line beside it, so the ops head drills from Gurugram to its top-selling biryani in two clicks.
lessons.tryit: In Tableau, create a calculated field SalesPerCustomer = { FIXED [Customer ID] : SUM([Sales]) }. Build a bar chart of Region (Columns) vs Sales (Rows), place it and a monthly trend line on one dashboard, then add a Filter action so clicking a region bar filters the trend line.

mistakes.m: Treating 'dimension vs measure' and 'discrete (blue) vs continuous (green)' as the same thing.
mistakes.fix: Data role (dimension/measure) and display type (discrete/continuous) are independent; a date dimension can be continuous green, and you can convert a pill's type when needed.

mistakes.m: Expecting { FIXED [Customer] : SUM([Sales]) } to change when you add more dimensions to the view.
mistakes.fix: FIXED locks granularity to the dimensions inside its own braces and ignores the view; use INCLUDE if you want it to also account for the view's dimensions.

mistakes.m: Cramming ten sheets and a five-slice pie onto one dashboard.
mistakes.fix: Choose bars for comparison and lines for trends, and keep sheets and marks few so the dashboard stays fast and readable.

links.t: Dimensions and measures, field roles (Tableau Help)

quiz.q: By default, when you drag a numeric Measure like Sales onto Rows, Tableau will:
opts: Show each individual value as a separate blue header
Aggregate it (SUM) and draw it as a continuous green axis
Treat it as a dimension that slices the view
Refuse to plot it until you write a calculation
quiz.why: Measures default to a continuous, green role and are automatically aggregated, SUM unless changed, producing an axis. Dimensions, by contrast, default to discrete blue pills that create headers.
quiz.approach: Read pill colour and shape at a glance: green/continuous measures make axes, blue/discrete dimensions make headers; adjust the role only when the view needs it.

quiz.q: What does { FIXED [Customer ID] : SUM([Sales]) } compute?
opts: Sales for whichever dimensions are currently in the view
Total sales per customer, regardless of the view's granularity
A running total of sales over time
Sales only for the selected customer in a filter
quiz.why: A FIXED LOD computes at exactly the dimensions inside its braces, here Customer ID, and ignores the dimensions in the view. So it returns each customer's total sales no matter how the sheet is sliced.
quiz.approach: Use FIXED when a metric must stay at a chosen grain (like per-customer) even as you build different views around it.

quiz.q: On a dashboard, you want clicking a bar in the Region sheet to filter the Trend sheet. What do you add?
opts: A calculated field
A Filter action
A context filter on the data source
A parameter control
quiz.why: A Filter action passes the clicked selection as a filter to other sheets on the dashboard, which is exactly this interaction. A calculated field changes data, a context filter narrows a single sheet's data, and a parameter is a manual input, none of them wire one sheet's click to another sheet.
quiz.approach: Make dashboards interactive with actions, Filter to drill, Highlight to focus, URL to jump out, rather than static side-by-side charts.

lessons.title: Model First: Star Schemas and the Measure-vs-Column Decision
lessons.concept:
Before you write a single DAX formula, the data model decides whether your report is fast and correct or slow and wrong. The professional pattern is the star schema: one or more fact tables (the events you measure — bills, orders, UPI payments) surrounded by dimension tables (the descriptive lookups — Date, Product, Store, Customer). Facts are tall and skinny: mostly numbers plus foreign keys. Dimensions are short and wide: one unique key plus lots of attributes to slice by. Relationships flow one-to-many, from the 'one' side (a dimension) to the 'many' side (the fact), and by default filter travels in a single direction. When you instead dump everything into one giant flat Excel-style table, filters stop propagating cleanly, totals double-count, and the file bloats.

The single most common intermediate confusion is measure versus calculated column. A calculated column is computed row by row at data-refresh, physically stored in the model (it eats RAM), and it has row context — it knows about 'this row'. Use a column when the value must be sliced, grouped, or placed on an axis, e.g. a Price Band label. A measure is computed at query time, stored nowhere, and evaluated in filter context — the set of filters coming from slicers and the visual's rows and columns. Use a measure for anything you aggregate that must react to user selections: Total Sales, Margin %. Rule of thumb: if it belongs on an axis or slicer, make a column; if it is a number in the Values well, make a measure.

There is also a performance reason to care. Power BI's VertiPaq engine compresses each column, and low-cardinality integer keys compress beautifully while high-cardinality text (a 15-digit bill number stored as text) bloats the model. So prefer integer surrogate keys on relationships, keep a dedicated Date table, and resist the temptation to store as a column what a measure can compute on the fly.
lessons.example: A DMart-style retailer keeps Fact_Sales (BillID, DateKey, StoreKey, ProductKey, Qty, Amount) with ~5 crore rows, and small dimensions Dim_Store (StoreKey, City, Region), Dim_Product (ProductKey, Category, Brand) and Dim_Date. 'Total Sales' is a measure = SUM(Fact_Sales[Amount]); a 'Price Band' bucket used in a slicer is a calculated column. Put Region on the axis and the Total Sales measure in Values, and the numbers stay correct as users click filters.
lessons.code:
-- Calculated column (stored, has row context, used on a slicer)
Price Band =
VAR unitPrice = DIVIDE( Fact_Sales[Amount], Fact_Sales[Qty] )
RETURN
SWITCH( TRUE(),
    unitPrice < 100,  "Under ₹100",
    unitPrice < 500,  "₹100–500",
    "₹500+" )

-- Measures (computed at query time, react to filters)
Total Sales = SUM( Fact_Sales[Amount] )
Margin %    = DIVIDE( [Total Sales] - [Total Cost], [Total Sales] )
lessons.tryit: Load two CSVs into Power BI — a sales file and a store master. Create a one-to-many relationship on StoreKey, write a 'Total Sales' measure, and add one calculated column that buckets stores into a Region Tier. Put Region on a bar chart's axis and Total Sales in Values, then confirm the total changes correctly when you slice by Region Tier.

mistakes.m: Computing Total Sales as a calculated column so you can 'see the number in the table'.
mistakes.fix: Totals belong in measures. A calculated column stores a per-row value and won't respond to slicers the way an aggregate should — and it wastes memory. Use SUM(...) in a measure.

mistakes.m: Flattening every table into one wide sheet, Excel-style.
mistakes.fix: Split into fact plus dimension tables (a star schema) so single-direction filters propagate correctly and VertiPaq compression works on the narrow dimension keys.

mistakes.m: Turning on bidirectional cross-filtering everywhere to 'make filtering work'.
mistakes.fix: Keep relationships single-direction from dimension to fact. Bidirectional filtering invites ambiguity, wrong totals, and slow queries — enable it only for a specific, understood need.

quiz.q: You want to bucket sales into 'Under ₹100 / ₹100–500 / ₹500+' and use those buckets as a slicer. What should you build?
opts: A calculated column
A measure
A separate fact table
A bidirectional relationship
quiz.why: Slicers and axes need a value that exists per row and is stored in the model — that is a calculated column. A measure produces an aggregate at query time and cannot be dropped onto a slicer as a groupable field.
quiz.approach: Axis or slicer → calculated column; a number in the Values well → measure.

quiz.q: Which statement about a DAX measure is correct?
opts: It is stored in the model and consumes RAM per row
It is evaluated in filter context at query time
It has row context by default
It must be created inside a dimension table
quiz.why: A measure is not stored; it is recomputed whenever a visual asks for it, using the filter context supplied by slicers and the visual's rows/columns. Row context appears in calculated columns and iterators, not in a bare measure.
quiz.approach: Reach for measures for anything that must react to user selections — they are cheap on memory and always current.

quiz.q: What is the healthiest relationship design for a star schema?
opts: Many-to-many between fact tables
One-to-many from each dimension to the fact, single direction
Bidirectional filtering on every relationship
One flat table with no relationships
quiz.why: Dimensions sit on the 'one' side and the fact on the 'many' side, with filters flowing single-direction from dimension to fact. This keeps totals correct and queries fast, avoiding the ambiguity that many-to-many and blanket bidirectional filtering create.
quiz.approach: Default to single-direction one-to-many; only deviate deliberately when a proven requirement demands it.

lessons.title: CALCULATE, Filter Context, and Iterators: How DAX Actually Thinks
lessons.concept:
Almost every DAX misunderstanding comes from mixing up two contexts. Filter context is the set of filters currently applied to the model — from slicers, page filters, and the rows and columns of the visual you are looking at. Row context is 'the current row', and it exists only inside calculated columns and iterator functions. A plain SUM sees only filter context; it does not loop over rows itself — the VertiPaq engine does the aggregation in bulk. Hold these two ideas apart and DAX stops feeling like magic.

CALCULATE is the most important function in the language: it evaluates an expression in a modified filter context. Written CALCULATE(expression, filter1, filter2, …), each filter argument by default overrides the existing filter on the columns it touches. So CALCULATE([Total Sales], Dim_Store[State] = "Maharashtra") returns Maharashtra sales even if the slicer is on Karnataka, because the argument replaces the State filter. CALCULATE also performs context transition: when called inside a row context, it converts the current row into an equivalent filter context — which is why measures behave sensibly inside iterators.

Two more tools complete the intermediate toolkit. ALL removes filters: ALL(a column) or ALL(a table) returns every row ignoring the current filter, which is exactly what a '% of total' needs — divide the filtered value by the all-context value. REMOVEFILTERS (Power BI 2019 and later) is a clearer-named modifier for the same 'clear the filter' intent. Iterators — SUMX, AVERAGEX, and friends — create a row context, evaluate an expression once per row, then aggregate. SUMX(Sales, Sales[Qty] * Sales[UnitPrice]) multiplies quantity by price on each row and only then sums; a plain SUM cannot do that because the multiplication is row-level and no such column exists yet.
lessons.example: A Zomato-style marketplace wants each restaurant's share of its city's sales. The numerator is the restaurant's sales in the current filter context; the denominator is city sales with the restaurant filter removed. REMOVEFILTERS (or ALL) clears the right filter so the ratio is correct as users drill from city to restaurant.
lessons.code:
Total Sales = SUM( Fact_Sales[Amount] )

-- Per-row revenue then summed (needs an iterator)
Revenue = SUMX( Fact_Sales, Fact_Sales[Qty] * Fact_Sales[UnitPrice] )

-- Override the slicer for one state
Sales Maharashtra = CALCULATE( [Total Sales], Dim_Store[State] = "Maharashtra" )

-- Share of the city's total
% of City =
DIVIDE(
    [Total Sales],
    CALCULATE( [Total Sales], REMOVEFILTERS( Dim_Store[Restaurant] ) )
)
lessons.tryit: Write a '% of Grand Total' measure using CALCULATE with ALL, and a SUMX-based Revenue measure. Compare the SUMX result against SUM of a stored Amount column to confirm they match when Amount already equals Qty × UnitPrice — and to see why SUMX is required when that column does not exist.

mistakes.m: Treating SUM and SUMX as interchangeable.
mistakes.fix: SUM aggregates one existing column. SUMX iterates row by row, evaluating an expression (like Qty × Price) before summing. Use SUMX when the value you need is not already a stored column.

mistakes.m: Believing CALCULATE always adds to the existing filters.
mistakes.fix: By default CALCULATE's filter arguments replace the filter on the columns they reference. Wrap the filter in KEEPFILTERS if you want it to intersect with, rather than override, the current context.

mistakes.m: Using ALL('WholeTable') when you only meant to clear one column.
mistakes.fix: ALL(Table) strips filters from every column of that table. To remove the filter from a single column, pass that column — ALL(Dim_Store[City]) — so other filters survive.

quiz.q: A slicer already filters Dim_Store[State] to Karnataka. You write CALCULATE([Total Sales], Dim_Store[State] = "Maharashtra"). What is the resulting filter on State?
opts: Karnataka AND Maharashtra (intersection, so blank)
Maharashtra — CALCULATE's argument replaces the slicer's filter
An error, because two filters conflict
Karnataka — the slicer always wins
quiz.why: By default a CALCULATE filter argument overrides the existing filter context on the columns it references, so the State filter becomes Maharashtra regardless of the slicer. Only KEEPFILTERS would make the two intersect.
quiz.approach: Read CALCULATE as 'evaluate this expression, but replace the filters on these columns' — override is the default, intersection is opt-in.

quiz.q: You need to sum Qty × UnitPrice across rows where that product doesn't exist as a stored column. Which function fits?
opts: SUM
SUMX
CALCULATE
AVERAGE
quiz.why: SUMX creates a row context, computes Qty × UnitPrice on each row, then adds the results. SUM can only aggregate a single existing column and cannot perform the per-row multiplication first.
quiz.approach: When the value must be built row by row before aggregating, reach for an X-iterator (SUMX, AVERAGEX, MAXX).

quiz.q: In a '% of category total' measure, which function removes the category filter so the denominator is the full total?
opts: ALL
RELATED
USERELATIONSHIP
EARLIER
quiz.why: ALL (or the clearer REMOVEFILTERS) strips filters from the specified column or table, giving the all-context total for the denominator. RELATED fetches a value across a relationship, USERELATIONSHIP activates an inactive relationship, and EARLIER references an outer row context — none of them clear filters.
quiz.approach: For part-to-whole ratios, put the filtered value over CALCULATE(value, ALL/REMOVEFILTERS(the column you want ignored)).

lessons.title: Time Intelligence, Visual Choice, and Keeping Reports Fast
lessons.concept:
Time intelligence — YTD, MTD, year-over-year — feels hard until you realise it all rests on one prerequisite: a dedicated Date table with continuous, gap-free dates, marked as a Date table in the model. Once that exists, the built-in functions do the heavy lifting. TOTALYTD([Total Sales], Dim_Date[Date]) accumulates from the start of the year to the current date. Because India's financial year runs April to March, you pass a year-end date so YTD aligns to the FY instead of the calendar year. SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR shifts the current date filter back exactly one year, which is all you need for a clean YoY growth measure.

Choosing the right visual is a skill, not a decoration. Match the chart to the question. Comparing a value across categories → a sorted bar or column chart. A trend over time → a line chart. Part-to-whole with only a few slices → a stacked bar or donut, used sparingly; avoid pie charts once you have more than about five slices because humans compare lengths far better than angles. Correlation between two measures → a scatter plot. A single headline number → a card. Row-level lookup → a table or matrix. The classic intermediate mistake is a 3-D pie with a dozen slices; the classic upgrade is a sorted bar chart with clutter removed.

Performance is mostly a set of habits. Prefer measures over calculated columns, keep a star schema, and reduce cardinality on keys. Use VAR to compute a value once and reuse it instead of repeating an expensive sub-expression. Remember that every visual on a page fires its own query, so twenty visuals mean twenty queries — trim the page, or pre-aggregate a huge fact table. Avoid bidirectional relationships, and use integer keys so VertiPaq compresses well. On a 10-crore-row banking transaction table, these choices are the difference between a snappy dashboard and a spinning wheel.
lessons.example: An AMFI-reporting mutual-fund AMC reports on the Indian financial year (April–March). 'Sales YTD (FY)' accumulates from 1 April, 'Sales LY' pulls the same period a year earlier, and 'YoY %' compares them. A line chart of monthly inflows over 24 months makes the seasonality obvious in a way a table never could.
lessons.code:
Total Sales = SUM( Fact_Sales[Amount] )

-- Year-to-date aligned to Indian FY (year-end = 31 March).
-- The year-end date is written month-day; verify it against your report locale.
Sales YTD (FY) = TOTALYTD( [Total Sales], Dim_Date[Date], "03-31" )

-- Same period one year ago
Sales LY = CALCULATE( [Total Sales], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR( Dim_Date[Date] ) )

-- Year-over-year growth using VAR to compute each side once
YoY % =
VAR cur = [Total Sales]
VAR ly  = [Sales LY]
RETURN DIVIDE( cur - ly, ly )
lessons.tryit: Create a Date table covering every day of your data's range, mark it as a Date table, and build 'Sales YTD (FY)' (year-end 31 March) plus a 'YoY %' measure. Put month on a line chart and confirm YTD resets in April, not January.

mistakes.m: Running time-intelligence off the fact table's own date column.
mistakes.fix: Build a separate, continuous Date table and mark it as a Date table. Functions like SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR need a complete, gap-free calendar to shift dates correctly.

mistakes.m: Leaving YTD on the calendar year for Indian reporting.
mistakes.fix: Pass the fiscal year-end date (31 March) to TOTALYTD/DATESYTD, or use a fiscal calendar, so year-to-date follows the April–March financial year.

mistakes.m: Cramming twenty visuals onto one page, then blaming Power BI for lag.
mistakes.fix: Each visual issues its own query. Reduce the number of visuals, lean on measures and a star schema, and consider aggregation tables for very large facts.

quiz.q: What does SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR(Dim_Date[Date]) do?
opts: Shifts the current date filter back exactly one year
Removes all date filters from the model
Computes a running cumulative total
Works without any Date table
quiz.why: It returns the set of dates in the current filter context shifted back by one year, so wrapping it in CALCULATE gives you the prior-year value for the same period. It relies on a proper contiguous Date table to do the shift.
quiz.approach: For YoY, take the current measure and CALCULATE it over SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR of your Date column.

quiz.q: What is the essential prerequisite for reliable time intelligence in Power BI?
opts: A measure literally named YTD
A dedicated, continuous Date table marked as a date table
Bidirectional relationships on every table
At least one pie chart on the page
quiz.why: Time-intelligence functions walk a calendar to find prior periods and year boundaries, so they require a gap-free Date table that Power BI recognises as the date table. Naming a measure or adding charts does nothing for the underlying date logic.
quiz.approach: Build and mark the Date table first; every YTD/MTD/YoY measure then becomes a one-liner.

quiz.q: You want to show monthly revenue across the last 24 months. Which visual is best?
opts: A pie chart
A sorted bar chart
A line chart
A single KPI card
quiz.why: A line chart is purpose-built for a value moving over time — it reveals trend and seasonality across the 24 points. A pie shows part-to-whole (not time), a bar is better for comparing categories, and a card collapses everything to one number.
quiz.approach: Trend over time → line; comparison across categories → sorted bar; one headline number → card.

lessons.title: Tableau Fluency: Dimensions vs Measures, FIXED LOD, and Actions
lessons.concept:
Tableau's whole mental model rests on two distinctions: dimensions versus measures, and discrete (blue) versus continuous (green). Dimensions are qualitative fields — Region, Category, Order Date — that slice the data and define the granularity of the view; by default they are discrete and blue, producing headers and panes. Measures are quantitative fields — Sales, Profit — that Tableau aggregates (SUM by default) and are continuous and green, producing an axis. Roles are not permanent: you can convert a field, and you can use a date discretely (Year headers) or continuously (a smooth time axis). The set of dimensions you place on a sheet is exactly what sets its level of detail.

Level of Detail (LOD) expressions let you compute an aggregate at a granularity different from the view, and FIXED is the workhorse. {FIXED [Customer ID] : SUM([Sales])} computes each customer's total sales regardless of what other fields sit on the sheet — ideal for lifetime value shown against monthly rows, cohort analysis, or first-purchase dates. The crucial subtlety is order of operations: a FIXED expression is computed before ordinary dimension filters, so a normal quick filter will not change its result. It does respond to context filters, so if you need the FIXED value to honour a filter, promote that filter to a context filter. INCLUDE and EXCLUDE, by contrast, compute relative to the dimensions already in the view.

Dashboards turn individual sheets into an interactive tool, and actions are the wiring. A Filter action lets a click on one sheet filter the others — click a state on a map and the KPI cards and trend line update. Highlight actions dim everything except the selection, parameter and set actions let a click change an input or membership, and URL actions jump out to an external page. The habit of strong dashboards is to pick one 'driver' sheet and let the others respond, so exploration flows from a single, obvious point of interaction.
lessons.example: A Flipkart-style seller-analytics dashboard uses {FIXED [Customer ID] : SUM([Sales])} to show each buyer's lifetime value repeated beside their monthly orders. A map of India by state sits next to a category bar chart; a Filter action makes clicking Maharashtra on the map instantly filter the category chart and the KPI cards to that state.
lessons.tryit: In Tableau, create {FIXED [Customer ID] : SUM([Sales])} and drop it on a view that also has Order Month — notice each customer's lifetime total repeats across their months. Then build a dashboard with a state map and a category bar chart, and add a Filter action so clicking a state drives the bar chart.

mistakes.m: Treating blue vs green as merely a colour theme.
mistakes.fix: Blue means discrete (headers and panes); green means continuous (an axis). The colour signals how Tableau lays out the field and whether it is aggregated — switching it changes the visualization, not just the look.

mistakes.m: Expecting a normal dimension quick filter to change a FIXED LOD result.
mistakes.fix: FIXED is computed before dimension filters in Tableau's order of operations. If the FIXED value must respect a filter, add that field to Context filters so it runs earlier.

mistakes.m: Building several disconnected dashboards to show related views.
mistakes.fix: Place the sheets on one dashboard and add a Filter action, so a click on the driver sheet interactively filters the others instead of forcing users to switch pages.

links.t: Tableau: dimensions, measures, and field roles

quiz.q: What does {FIXED [Region] : SUM([Sales])} return?
opts: Total sales per region, ignoring the other dimensions in the view
Sales aggregated at whatever granularity the view currently shows
A field that removes Region from the analysis
Null unless Region is on the sheet
quiz.why: A FIXED LOD computes its aggregate at the stated level of detail (Region) regardless of the other fields on the sheet, so you get each region's total even when the view is broken down further. That independence from the view is exactly what FIXED provides.
quiz.approach: Use FIXED when you need an aggregate pinned to a specific granularity that the current view would otherwise change.

quiz.q: A green (continuous) pill on Rows or Columns in Tableau typically produces what?
opts: Discrete headers and panes
A continuous axis
A quick filter
A set
quiz.why: Green denotes continuous fields, which Tableau draws as an axis with a smooth range of values. Blue (discrete) fields instead produce distinct headers and panes; the pill colour tells you the layout before you even look at the chart.
quiz.approach: Read pill colour first: green = continuous axis, blue = discrete headers — it predicts how the view will be built.

quiz.q: On a dashboard, you want clicking a bar in one sheet to filter another sheet. What do you use?
opts: A calculated field
A Filter action
A FIXED LOD expression
A parameter alone
quiz.why: A Filter action passes the selection from a source sheet as a filter to target sheets, creating the click-to-filter interactivity. Calculated fields and LOD expressions shape values, and a parameter by itself does not pass a selection between sheets.
quiz.approach: For click-driven interactivity across sheets, add a Filter action and pick one sheet as the driver.

lessons.title: CALCULATE and Filter Context: The Engine Room of DAX
lessons.concept:
Every number in a Power BI visual is the answer to a hidden question. When you build a matrix with State on rows and Month on columns and drop in Total Sales = SUM(Sales[Amount]), the cell at (Maharashtra, April) is not the grand total. Before summing, the engine silently filtered the fact table to rows where State = Maharashtra AND Month = April. That invisible set of filters — arriving from row headers, column headers, slicers, the page filter and cross-highlighting from other visuals — is the filter context. Your measure never mentions Maharashtra, yet it 'knows' about it because it is evaluated inside that context. Learning DAX is really learning to see this invisible context and reshape it.

CALCULATE is the one function that can change the filter context: CALCULATE(expression, filter1, filter2, ...). The rule that trips up every beginner: a simple boolean filter such as Sales[Channel] = "Online" REPLACES whatever filter already sits on that column — it does not add to it. This is exactly what makes 'percent of total' work: you divide the value in the current context by the same value with the column's filter removed, using REMOVEFILTERS (the modern, clearer name) or ALL. When you genuinely want to add a condition on top of the user's selection instead of overriding it, wrap it in KEEPFILTERS so the two conditions intersect.

The deep magic is context transition. When a measure is used inside a row context — a calculated column, or an iterator like SUMX — the measure carries an implicit CALCULATE that converts the current row into a filter. This is why a lifetime-value calculation must be wrapped in CALCULATE: without it, a bare SUM over a related table just returns the grand total on every single row. 'Same total repeated on every row' is the signature symptom of a missing context transition.
lessons.example: A DMart-style grocery chain builds a matrix with City on rows and Total Sales in the values: Mumbai shows Rs 4.2 crore, Pune Rs 1.8 crore. To add a '% of All-India' column, the analyst divides each city's Total Sales by CALCULATE([Total Sales], REMOVEFILTERS(Store[City])) — the Rs 50 crore national total — so Mumbai reads 8.4%. The measure text is completely generic; the matrix row supplies the per-city filter context, and REMOVEFILTERS lifts only the City filter for the denominator while every other filter (say a selected month) stays intact.
lessons.code:
Total Sales = SUM(Sales[Amount])

-- A boolean filter REPLACES the existing filter on Channel
Online Sales = CALCULATE([Total Sales], Sales[Channel] = "Online")

-- % of All-India: clear only the City filter for the denominator
% of All-India =
VAR ThisCity = [Total Sales]
VAR AllIndia = CALCULATE([Total Sales], REMOVEFILTERS(Store[City]))
RETURN DIVIDE(ThisCity, AllIndia)

-- Context transition: convert the current row into a filter
Lifetime Value = CALCULATE(SUM(Sales[Amount]))   -- in a Customer calc column, per-customer
-- Without CALCULATE, SUM(Sales[Amount]) returns the grand total on every row
lessons.tryit: Build a matrix with Store[City] on rows and add a '% of All-India' measure using DIVIDE([Total Sales], CALCULATE([Total Sales], REMOVEFILTERS(Store[City]))). Confirm the percentages add up to 100%. Then create a second version using ALL(Store) instead of REMOVEFILTERS(Store[City]) and observe how removing filters from the whole table (not just City) changes the denominator.

mistakes.m: Thinking CALCULATE(SUM(Sales[Amount]), Sales[Region] = "South") adds 'South' on top of the Region filter already in the visual.
mistakes.fix: A boolean filter on a column REPLACES the existing filter on that column. If the visual is showing 'North', the measure still returns South. To intersect with the user's selection instead, wrap it: KEEPFILTERS(Sales[Region] = "South").

mistakes.m: Reaching for ALL(Sales) (the whole table) when you only meant to clear one column, wiping filters you needed and over-broadening the denominator.
mistakes.fix: Target the specific column: REMOVEFILTERS(Store[City]) clears City only; ALLEXCEPT keeps the columns you name. Remove exactly the filters your calculation needs to ignore, nothing more.

mistakes.m: Expecting SUM(Sales[Amount]) in a calculated column on the Customer table to give each customer's own total.
mistakes.fix: A bare SUM in a calculated column has no context transition, so it returns the grand total on every row. Wrap it in CALCULATE(SUM(Sales[Amount])) so the current customer row becomes a filter that propagates through the relationship.

quiz.q: In a matrix with State on rows, Total Sales = SUM(Sales[Amount]) shows Rs 4.2 crore for Maharashtra. Why does a measure that never mentions Maharashtra return a state-specific number?
opts: Because SUM automatically groups by the first column in the table
Because the row's State = Maharashtra becomes part of the filter context in which the measure is evaluated
Because measures are recalculated and stored per row like calculated columns
Because Power BI sorts the data by State before summing
quiz.why: Every visual cell supplies a filter context. Here the row header adds State = Maharashtra (on top of any slicers or page filters), and the measure is evaluated inside that context, so SUM only sees Maharashtra's rows. The measure text stays generic; the context does the narrowing.
quiz.approach: Read every measure as 'this expression, evaluated in whatever filter context the visual creates.' Keep measures context-agnostic and let the report supply the slicing.

quiz.q: You write Sales % = DIVIDE([Total Sales], CALCULATE([Total Sales], Sales[Region] = "South")). In a visual already filtered to Region = North, what does the denominator return?
opts: Sales for North, because existing filters always win
Sales for North and South combined
Sales for South, because the boolean filter replaces the existing Region filter
An error, because you cannot filter a column already used in the visual
quiz.why: A boolean filter argument to CALCULATE overwrites any existing filter on the same column, so it discards 'North' and sets Region = South. To intersect (North AND South, which would be empty here) you would wrap it in KEEPFILTERS. This replace-on-same-column behaviour is exactly what makes 'percent of a fixed segment' calculations possible.
quiz.approach: Remember CALCULATE's default is REPLACE on the same column. Use KEEPFILTERS when you want to add a condition on top of the user's selection instead of overriding it.

quiz.q: A calculated column on the Customer table, Lifetime Value = SUM(Sales[Amount]), shows the same Rs 50 crore grand total on every customer row. What fixes it?
opts: Change SUM to SUMX
Wrap it in CALCULATE: CALCULATE(SUM(Sales[Amount])) so context transition filters Sales to the current customer
Sort the Customer table by CustomerID first
It is impossible in a calculated column; only measures can do this
quiz.why: In a calculated column there is a row context (the current customer) but no filter context, and SUM does no context transition, so it sums all sales. Wrapping in CALCULATE performs context transition: the current customer row becomes a filter that propagates through the relationship, giving that customer's own total.
quiz.approach: When an aggregation sits inside a row context, add CALCULATE to trigger context transition. 'Same total on every row' is the classic signature of a missing context transition.

lessons.title: Time Intelligence That Actually Works: YTD, MTD and Year-over-Year
lessons.concept:
Before you write a single YTD measure, build a proper Date table: one continuous row per day covering every date in your data, with no gaps, then mark it (Table tools > Mark as date table). This is non-negotiable. Time-intelligence functions like TOTALYTD and SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR literally walk the calendar day by day; if 15-August is missing because nothing sold that day, or you point the function at the date column inside the Sales fact table, the walk hits a hole and quietly returns wrong numbers. A dedicated Date table also lets you encode what Indian businesses actually need — a fiscal year starting 1 April, festival flags for Diwali and Dussehra weeks, quarter labels — as clean columns you can slice by.

For running totals, TOTALYTD(SUM(Sales[Amount]), 'Date'[Date]) accumulates from the start of the year up to the current context date. By default that means the calendar year ending 31 December. To match the Indian fiscal year, pass the optional year-end-date argument in month/day form: TOTALYTD([Total Sales], 'Date'[Date], "3/31") resets the accumulation each 1 April, running April through March. TOTALMTD does the same for month-to-date. The month/day string is what Microsoft recommends because it resolves unambiguously across locales.

For comparisons, SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR('Date'[Date]) returns a table of the current dates shifted exactly one year back; feeding that table into CALCULATE re-evaluates your base measure over last year's equivalent period. From there, year-over-year growth is just (this year minus last year) over last year. DATEADD is the flexible cousin: DATEADD('Date'[Date], -1, MONTH) shifts back one month, -1, QUARTER shifts a quarter, and so on. The beauty is that all of these track whatever period the user selects — no hardcoded years to break next April.
lessons.example: A Pune apparel retailer closes its books on 31 March. In April 2025 (FY26) a dashboard card should show YTD sales that reset each 1 April and a YoY % against FY25. Using TOTALYTD([Total Sales], 'Date'[Date], "3/31") for the running total and CALCULATE([Total Sales], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR('Date'[Date])) for the comparison, April FY26 sales of Rs 92 lakh line up against April FY25's Rs 80 lakh — a +15% YoY — and both numbers re-shift automatically as the user drags the month slicer.
lessons.code:
Total Sales = SUM(Sales[Amount])

-- Fiscal-year YTD (April to March). "3/31" is month/day format.
Sales YTD (FY) = TOTALYTD([Total Sales], 'Date'[Date], "3/31")

-- Same period last year
Sales LY = CALCULATE([Total Sales], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR('Date'[Date]))

-- Year-over-year growth %
YoY % = DIVIDE([Total Sales] - [Sales LY], [Sales LY])

-- Previous month, the flexible way
Sales PM = CALCULATE([Total Sales], DATEADD('Date'[Date], -1, MONTH))
lessons.tryit: Create a Date table with CALENDAR(DATE(2019,4,1), DATE(2026,3,31)), add a Fiscal Year column (April-March), and mark it as a Date table. Relate its Date column to Sales. Then build a line chart of Total Sales versus Sales LY by month, and a card showing YoY %. Drag a Fiscal Year slicer and confirm both series shift together.

mistakes.m: Pointing time-intelligence functions at the date column inside the Sales fact table instead of a dedicated Date table.
mistakes.fix: Build a separate, contiguous Date table (one row per day, no gaps), relate it to Sales, and mark it as a Date table. Fact-table dates skip days with no transactions, and those gaps silently break YTD and SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR.

mistakes.m: Reporting YTD as January-December when the business actually closes its books on 31 March.
mistakes.fix: Pass the year-end-date argument in month/day form: TOTALYTD([Total Sales], 'Date'[Date], "3/31"), or define an explicit Fiscal Year column, so the accumulation runs April through March.

mistakes.m: Comparing to 'last year' by hardcoding a number or filtering Year = 2024, which breaks the moment the slicer changes.
mistakes.fix: Use SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR (or DATEADD(..., -1, YEAR)) inside CALCULATE. It shifts with whatever period the user selects, so the comparison never goes stale.

quiz.q: Why must time-intelligence functions run off a dedicated Date table rather than the date column in the Sales fact table?
opts: The Sales table's date column is stored as text, not a date
Time-intelligence functions need a continuous, gap-free set of dates to walk; the fact table skips days with no transactions
Fact tables cannot participate in relationships
DAX cannot read dates that live in a fact table
quiz.why: Functions like TOTALYTD and SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR navigate the calendar day by day. If a day had no sales it is simply absent from the fact table, so the walk hits a hole and returns wrong results. A dedicated Date table guarantees one continuous row per day, and marking it as a date table tells the engine to trust it for this navigation.
quiz.approach: Always model a separate, contiguous Date dimension and mark it as such. It is the backbone of every time-intelligence calculation.

quiz.q: An Indian retailer closes its books on 31 March. Which measure correctly accumulates YTD from April through March?
opts: TOTALYTD([Total Sales], 'Date'[Date])
TOTALYTD([Total Sales], 'Date'[Date], "3/31")
CALCULATE([Total Sales], DATESYTD('Date'[Date], "12/31"))
TOTALYTD([Total Sales], Sales[OrderDate], "4/1")
quiz.why: The optional year-end-date argument (month/day) tells YTD where the fiscal year ends. '3/31' makes the accumulation reset each 1 April, matching the Indian fiscal year. Option 0 defaults to 31 December; option 2 explicitly ends 31 December; option 3 both uses the fact-table date and passes '4/1' (the fiscal-year START, not its end).
quiz.approach: For fiscal-year reporting, supply the year-end date in month/day form to the *YTD functions, and always drive them off the Date dimension.

quiz.q: Sales LY = CALCULATE([Total Sales], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR('Date'[Date])). What does SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR return, and how does CALCULATE use it?
opts: A single number (last year's total) that CALCULATE displays directly
A table of dates shifted one year earlier, which CALCULATE applies as a filter so [Total Sales] is re-evaluated over that shifted period
A boolean that is TRUE for last-year rows
A change to the relationship between the Date and Sales tables
quiz.why: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR is a table function: it returns the current context's dates, each moved back twelve months. CALCULATE takes that table as a filter argument, so Total Sales is recomputed over last year's equivalent dates. That is why it automatically tracks whatever period the visual is showing.
quiz.approach: Think of time-intelligence functions as date-table generators fed into CALCULATE. The value comes from re-evaluating your base measure over the returned set of dates.

lessons.title: Iterators and a Model That Flies: SUMX, Measures vs Columns, and Star-Schema Performance
lessons.concept:
Decide between a measure and a calculated column by asking when and where it is computed. A calculated column is computed once, row by row, at data refresh, and is stored in the model — it consumes memory and is materialised for every row. It lives in a row context and is the right home for an attribute you will slice, group or filter by, such as a Price Band label 'Budget / Mid / Premium'. A measure is computed at query time inside the filter context of each visual, is stored nowhere, and is the right tool for a number that must respond to slicers, such as Total Margin. The rule of thumb: if it goes on an axis or a slicer, it is probably a column; if it is a number that changes as you filter, it is a measure.

SUM takes exactly one column, so it cannot multiply two columns row by row. When revenue is Quantity times Unit Price and there is no stored line-total column, you need an iterator: SUMX(Sales, Sales[Qty] * Sales[UnitPrice]) walks the table in row context, computes the product on each row, then adds them up. This is the mathematically correct answer — SUM(Qty) * SUM(UnitPrice) is wrong, because the product of sums is not the sum of products. AVERAGEX, MAXX and RANKX follow the same iterate-then-aggregate pattern. Iterators are powerful, but they iterate, so keep the per-row expression cheap.

Performance in Power BI is mostly model shape plus column choices. The VertiPaq engine stores each column compressed and separately (it is columnar). Low-cardinality columns — few distinct values — compress beautifully; high-cardinality columns such as a unique transaction ID, a full second-level timestamp, or free-text notes barely compress and bloat both the file and every query. Prefer a star schema — one lean fact table surrounded by small dimension tables joined one-to-many — over a single giant flat table or a snowflake. Star schemas give the engine short, single-direction filter paths, let dimensions do the grouping while the fact does the summing, and keep cardinality low. Split a datetime into a Date key plus a Time key, drop columns you never use, and prefer measures over calculated columns whenever you do not need to slice by the value, so you are not permanently storing derived data.
lessons.example: An FMCG distributor loads 2 crore invoice lines that carry Qty and UnitPrice but no line-total column. Revenue must be SUMX(Sales, Sales[Qty] * Sales[UnitPrice]); SUM(Qty) * SUM(UnitPrice) would be nonsense at every subtotal. The 900 MB model shrinks sharply once the analyst splits the raw second-level invoice timestamp (2 crore distinct values, almost no compression) into a Date key and a Time key, and reshapes the data into a star schema with lean Product, Store and Date dimensions around the invoice fact.
lessons.code:
-- WRONG: product of sums is not the sum of products
Revenue (wrong) = SUM(Sales[Qty]) * SUM(Sales[UnitPrice])

-- RIGHT: iterate per line, then sum
Revenue = SUMX(Sales, Sales[Qty] * Sales[UnitPrice])

-- Weighted average selling price (iterator over the fact)
Avg Selling Price = DIVIDE(SUMX(Sales, Sales[Qty] * Sales[UnitPrice]), SUM(Sales[Qty]))

-- A calculated COLUMN: stored, low-cardinality, sliceable on a slicer
Price Band =
SWITCH(TRUE(),
    Sales[UnitPrice] < 500, "Budget",
    Sales[UnitPrice] < 2000, "Mid",
    "Premium")
lessons.tryit: Take a sales table with Qty and UnitPrice but no line-total column. Create Revenue two ways — SUM(Qty) * SUM(UnitPrice) and SUMX(Sales, Sales[Qty] * Sales[UnitPrice]) — put both in a matrix by Category and confirm only the SUMX version is correct at the subtotals. Then delete one high-cardinality column (a raw timestamp or a unique note field), save, and watch the .pbix file size drop.

mistakes.m: Storing a number that should react to slicers (like 'Total Sales') as a calculated column, bloating the model and often showing wrong totals.
mistakes.fix: Aggregations that must respond to filter context belong in measures, computed at query time. Reserve calculated columns for stable row-level attributes you group or slice by.

mistakes.m: Writing SUM(Sales[Qty]) * SUM(Sales[UnitPrice]) for revenue.
mistakes.fix: The multiplication must happen per row before summing. Use SUMX(Sales, Sales[Qty] * Sales[UnitPrice]). The product of sums is not the sum of products.

mistakes.m: Keeping one huge flat table full of high-cardinality columns (full timestamps, unique IDs, free text) and wondering why the report is slow and the file is enormous.
mistakes.fix: Model a star schema, split timestamps into Date and Time keys, drop unused columns, and lower cardinality where you can. VertiPaq compresses low-cardinality columns far more effectively.

links.t: Understand star schema and its importance for Power BI - Microsoft Learn
CALCULATE function (context transition in iterators) - Microsoft Learn

quiz.q: You need a 'Segment' label (Budget / Mid / Premium) to place on a slicer and group visuals by. Measure or calculated column?
opts: A measure, because measures are always faster
A calculated column, because you need to slice and group by the value and it is a fixed row-level attribute
Either one works identically
A calculated column, but only if the table has fewer than 1000 rows
quiz.why: Slicers and axes need a materialised, sliceable field, which is a calculated column (or better, a column built in Power Query). A measure produces a number inside a visual's context and cannot be dropped on a slicer to group rows. Because the segment is a stable per-row attribute, a column is its correct home.
quiz.approach: Ask 'Will I slice, group or filter by it?' If yes, make it a column. 'Is it a number that must react to filters?' If yes, make it a measure.

quiz.q: Revenue is Quantity times Unit Price per line. Which expression is correct across a Category subtotal?
opts: SUM(Sales[Qty]) * SUM(Sales[UnitPrice])
SUMX(Sales, Sales[Qty] * Sales[UnitPrice])
SUM(Sales[Qty] * Sales[UnitPrice])
CALCULATE(SUM(Sales[Qty]), Sales[UnitPrice])
quiz.why: Revenue is a sum of per-row products, so you must iterate: SUMX evaluates Qty times Price on each row, then adds the results. SUM accepts only a single column reference, so option 2 is not even valid DAX, and the product of the two column sums (option 0) is a different, wrong number.
quiz.approach: When a calculation multiplies or combines columns row by row before aggregating, reach for an X-iterator such as SUMX or AVERAGEX.

quiz.q: Your .pbix is 900 MB and sluggish. Which change helps the VertiPaq engine the most?
opts: Adding more calculated columns to precompute values
Merging every dimension into one wide flat table
Removing high-cardinality columns (raw timestamps, unique text IDs) and adopting a star schema
Converting all measures into calculated columns
quiz.why: VertiPaq stores each column compressed and separately, and compression works best on low-cardinality columns. Unique or high-cardinality columns barely compress and dominate the file. Removing unused high-cardinality columns and shaping the data as a star schema shrinks the model and shortens filter paths. The other options all add stored data or lengthen scans.
quiz.approach: Optimise by cutting cardinality and column count and by modelling a star schema, not by materialising still more derived data.

lessons.title: Tableau LOD Expressions and Dashboards That Talk Back: FIXED and Actions
lessons.concept:
Tableau's mental model is worth learning precisely. Fields split into Dimensions (qualitative things such as customer, city or order date; they slice the data and create headers) and Measures (numeric values such as sales or profit that get aggregated). Separately and independently, a pill is blue when it is discrete (it makes headers and labels) or green when it is continuous (it makes an axis). The granularity — the level of detail — of a view is set by whichever dimensions you drop on rows, columns or the Marks card, and Tableau then aggregates every measure to that level. Most of Tableau's power, and most of its confusion, comes from controlling that level of detail.

Level of Detail (LOD) expressions let a single number be computed at a granularity different from the view. {FIXED [Customer ID] : SUM([Sales])} computes each customer's total sales regardless of what else is in the view — ideal for 'what share of a customer's lifetime spend happened this quarter', for cohort analysis, or for flagging 'customers whose lifetime value exceeds Rs 1,00,000'. INCLUDE pulls in dimensions below the view level (for example, average sales per store even when the view is only by region); EXCLUDE strips a dimension out (for example, each sub-category as a percent of its category total). The crucial subtlety is timing: a FIXED expression is computed early, before dimension filters, so a normal filter on Region will NOT change a FIXED customer total. If you need that filter to reach a FIXED expression, promote it to a Context Filter, because context filters (along with extract and data-source filters) run before FIXED, whereas ordinary dimension filters, INCLUDE/EXCLUDE and measure filters all run after.

Dashboards become interactive through Actions. A Filter action lets a click on one sheet filter another — click a state on an India map and the bar chart narrows to that state's cities. A Highlight action dims everything except the related marks; a Go to URL action opens an external page with the clicked value; Parameter and Set actions push a selection into a parameter or a set to drive more advanced logic. Actions fire on hover, on select (click) or from a menu, and you choose what happens when the selection is cleared — keep all values, show none, or exclude all. A well-built dashboard uses one summary sheet as the controller that filters several detail sheets, turning a static report into a guided exploration.
lessons.example: A retail analyst builds a dashboard with an India state map and a city-level bar chart. A calculated field {FIXED [Customer ID] : SUM([Sales])} gives each customer's lifetime spend, and an IF flags anyone above Rs 1,00,000 as 'High Value'. A Filter action wires the map to the bar chart, so clicking Karnataka instantly narrows the cities to Bengaluru, Mysuru and Hubballi — a static report becomes a click-to-drill exploration, while the FIXED lifetime totals stay whole because the region click is a filter action feeding a separate view, not a change to each customer's fixed total.
lessons.tryit: In Tableau, create {FIXED [Customer ID] : SUM([Sales])}, build a High Value vs Regular segment from it, and count customers in each segment. Then place a State map and a City bar chart on one dashboard and add a Filter action so clicking a state filters the cities. Finally, add a Region filter, right-click it, choose 'Add to Context', and watch whether your FIXED totals change versus when it is a plain dimension filter.

mistakes.m: Expecting a normal dimension filter (say Region = South) to shrink a {FIXED} LOD total.
mistakes.fix: FIXED is computed before dimension filters in Tableau's order of operations, so it ignores them. To make a filter affect a FIXED expression, right-click it and choose 'Add to Context'; context filters run before FIXED.

mistakes.m: Confusing 'dimension vs measure' (the data role) with 'blue vs green' (discrete vs continuous).
mistakes.fix: They are two independent dials. A measure can be made discrete (blue) and a date dimension can be made continuous (green). Blue/green controls headers vs axis; dimension/measure controls slicing vs aggregating.

mistakes.m: Building a dashboard where users must set filters manually on each separate sheet.
mistakes.fix: Add a Filter action from one controller sheet so a single click filters all the related sheets, and deliberately set the 'clearing the selection' behaviour (show all values versus exclude all) so the reset state makes sense.

links.t: Level of Detail Expressions - Tableau Help
Tableau's Order of Operations - Tableau Help

quiz.q: {FIXED [Customer ID] : SUM([Sales])} gives each customer's lifetime sales. You then apply a normal dimension filter, Region = 'South'. What happens to the FIXED totals?
opts: They shrink to only South-region sales
They stay at full lifetime values, because FIXED is computed before dimension filters
They become blank
Tableau throws an order-of-operations error
quiz.why: In Tableau's order of operations, FIXED LOD expressions are computed before dimension filters, so an ordinary Region filter never reaches the FIXED calculation. If you need the filter to apply, promote it to a Context Filter, which runs earlier in the pipeline, ahead of FIXED.
quiz.approach: Know the pipeline: context filters, then FIXED, then dimension filters, then INCLUDE/EXCLUDE, then measure filters. Use a context filter whenever a filter must influence a FIXED expression.

quiz.q: In Tableau, what does the blue-versus-green colour of a pill indicate?
opts: Whether the field is a dimension (blue) or a measure (green)
Whether the field is discrete (blue, makes headers) or continuous (green, makes an axis)
Whether the field is currently filtered
Whether the field comes from an extract or a live connection
quiz.why: Colour encodes discrete (blue) versus continuous (green), which is independent of dimension versus measure. Discrete fields create headers and labels; continuous fields create a continuous axis. Dimensions default to discrete and measures to continuous, but you can convert either — for example, make a continuous green date to get a smooth timeline axis.
quiz.approach: Keep two separate dials in mind: role (dimension or measure = group vs aggregate) and type (discrete or continuous = headers vs axis).

quiz.q: You want clicking a state on a map to filter a separate city-level bar chart on the same dashboard. What do you add?
opts: A calculated field using FIXED
A parameter
A Filter action from the map sheet to the bar-chart sheet
A data source filter
quiz.why: A Filter action passes the selected mark's values (here, the state) as a filter to a target sheet, so clicking a state narrows the city chart. Parameters and data-source filters do not respond to marks clicked in another sheet, and a FIXED LOD changes granularity rather than creating cross-sheet interactivity.
quiz.approach: For click-to-filter interactivity across sheets, use dashboard Filter actions with a clear controller sheet and a deliberately chosen deselect behaviour.
